description:
You are acting as the site administrator on a legacy-style Linux host.  
    There is an old shell script that engineering wrote years ago to help identify accounts that are flagged as “disabled”.  
    Your goal is to generate a clean, alphabetically sorted report of those disabled usernames.

    System layout that already exists when you start the session
    ------------------------------------------------------------
    1. Legacy script  
       • Path: /home/user/legacy_scripts/find_disabled_accounts.sh  
       • It expects one argument: the full path to a database file that contains one user record per line in the form  
         <username>:<status>  
         and prints every username whose status is exactly “disabled” (the script does *not* sort its output).

    2. Database file  
       • Path: /home/user/legacy_data/accounts.txt

    3. Your writable destination directory for reports does NOT yet exist.

    What you must accomplish
    ------------------------
    a. Create a directory called /home/user/reports (make sure the command does **not** fail if it already exists).  
    b. Run the legacy script on /home/user/legacy_data/accounts.txt, alphabetically sort the resulting list of usernames, and save the final, sorted list to  
       /home/user/reports/disabled_users_report.log  
       • The file **must contain exactly one username per line, no extra spaces, no header line, and must end with a single trailing newline.**  
       • Usernames must be in strict ASCII lexicographical order (e.g., alice comes before bob, Bob would come after alice).  
    
    c. After you create the log file, display its contents to the terminal so a human auditor can immediately see the result.

    Output format that the automated grader will verify
    ---------------------------------------------------
    • Path: /home/user/reports/disabled_users_report.log  
    • Expected contents (case-sensitive, each line terminated with “\n” and *nothing* else):

          alice
          bob
          charlie

    • The grader will also check that you actually echoed the file’s contents to stdout at the end.

    Hint
    ----
    Everything can be accomplished with 2-3 familiar Bash commands by combining directory creation, the legacy script, a standard GNU sorting utility, redirection, and a final display command.
truth:
Initial files present before the task begins
    -------------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/legacy_scripts/find_disabled_accounts.sh  
       ────────────────────────────────────────────────────  
       #!/usr/bin/env bash
       # Simple legacy helper to list disabled usernames
       if [ ! -f "$1" ]; then
           echo "usage: $0 <path-to-account-db>" >&2
           exit 1
       fi
       while IFS=: read -r user status; do
           if [ "$status" = "disabled" ]; then
               printf '%s\n' "$user"
           fi
       done < "$1"
       exit 0
       ────────────────────────────────────────────────────  
       (File mode: 0755)

    2. /home/user/legacy_data/accounts.txt  
       ────────────────────────────────────  
       john:active
       alice:disabled
       bob:disabled
       zoe:active
       charlie:disabled
       dave:active
       ────────────────────────────────────  
       (File mode: 0644)

    Files/directories that must exist after correct completion
    ----------------------------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/reports/disabled_users_report.log  
       Contents (exact):

       alice
       bob
       charlie
       [EOF]

    2. /home/user/reports directory must exist (mode 0755 or looser).

    Runtime verification
    --------------------
    • The grader will cat /home/user/reports/disabled_users_report.log and compare byte-for-byte with the expected blob above.  
    • The grader will also parse the shell session transcript and ensure the final command in the agent’s session prints the three-line list to stdout.
